text: The laparoscope enters the abdomen, revealing a large perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer with visible contamination of gastric material throughout the abdominal cavity.
The laparoscopic view reveals significant peritonitis and accumulation of intra-abdominal fluid, indicative of the perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer.
The surgeon performs primary closure of the perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer using multiple intra-optic sutures with a tuboetabon, employing an intra-corporeal suturing technique.
The surgeon reinforces the primary closure of the perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer with a second layer of sutures, ensuring secure closure of the ulcer site.
The surgeon drains the intra-abdominal fluid and irrigates the abdominal cavity with an antibiotic solution, ensuring the area is clean and free of contaminants.
The surgeon mobilizes the omental attachments from the transverse colon to create an omental patch, which is used to reinforce the closure of the gastric ulcer.
The surgeon confirms the integrity of the gastric ulcer closure by inflating the stomach with a nasogastric tube, using diluted blue dye to check for any extravasation, which is absent.
The surgeon reinforces the area of the gastric ulcer repair by applying a patch of greater omentum over the closure site.
